title: 7.10 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 7.10!
topic_path: The Long Nineteenth Century (1750 to 1914 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What best explains why some Ghanaian laborers secretly dropped goods off of canoes during colonial rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They were resisting colonial power by redirecting goods and avoiding taxes.
They lacked the skills needed to transport goods safely by canoe.
They wanted to shrink the market for imported goods.
They were helping the colonial government collect more tax revenue.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What were lorries, and how did they help Ghanaians resist colonial control over trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Lorries were trucks that Ghanaians used to bypass British railways and move goods independently.
Lorries were taxes that Ghanaians charged on British goods.
Lorries were warehouses where goods were unloaded and taxed by colonial officials.
Lorries were boats used to secretly move goods past colonial tax authorities.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did British colonial officials refer to Ghanaian lorries as "pirates"?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Lorry drivers smuggled goods through unofficial routes, avoiding British control and taxation.
Lorries were used to attack British officials and steal their goods.
British law restricted transport to railways under their control, and lorries disrupted that system.
Lorries were operated by British soldiers to enforce colonial trade laws.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Ghanaian farmers differ from many other African farmers under colonial rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They maintained control over the sale and transport of their crops, like cocoa.
They depended entirely on British officials to set prices and distribute their crops.
They resisted colonization by refusing to grow cash crops.
They grew only food crops to feed their families and avoided colonial markets.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was a key reason Indians began to resist the rule of the British East India Company (EIC)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The EIC disrupted traditional economies and disrespected local customs.
The EIC encouraged Indian rulers to take control of British territory.
The EIC promoted democratic reforms that challenged Indian religious beliefs.
The EIC allowed Indian soldiers to rise through the ranks freely.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did a disagreement over ammunition contribute to resistance against the British East India Company in India?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Indian soldiers believed the cartridge grease disrespected their religious beliefs.
British commanders refused to give Indian soldiers modern weapons.
Indian soldiers were upset about being forced to manufacture ammunition.
The British threatened to cut off ammunition supplies during a food shortage.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What changed in India after the British Crown took over control of the colony from the East India Company in 1858?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The British Crown imposed tighter control, centralizing power and expanding surveillance.
The British Crown loosened its control and granted India more political autonomy.
Indian leaders were allowed to rule independently as long as they paid taxes to Britain.
India gained independence under Queen Victoria’s symbolic leadership.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were armed struggles against colonial powers not common?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonized people lacked access to the military resources needed to challenge powerful empires.
Colonized people usually accepted the rule of imperialists with little resistance.
Imperial powers used persuasive propaganda that earned them the loyalty of colonized people.
European empires governed fairly and met the needs of colonized populations.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did crops like cassava help colonized people resist imperial rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They helped people stay mobile and avoid imperial taxes and control.
They were highly profitable crops that funded local rebellions.
They symbolized cultural traditions and strengthened indigenous identity.
They poisoned imperial officials who didn’t know how to prepare them.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did some colonized people accommodate or adapt to imperial rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They had to be strategic in order to survive.
They had ethnic ties to European powers.
They hoped to gain full citizenship under imperial rule.
They had indigenous beliefs that prohibited resistance.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the French policy of educating people in its colonies ultimately backfire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Educated colonial subjects began to demand rights and independence.
Colonial subjects stopped speaking French and rejected European culture.
Colonial subjects migrated to France in such large numbers that it created economic problems.
Education programs failed to attract interest and were largely ignored.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below illustrates how the British media portrayed the 1857 Indian Rebellion. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**How does this cartoon reflect the British public’s view of the 1857 Indian Rebellion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The cartoon shows a tall, fierce-looking woman dressed in classical robes, representing Britain. She raises a sword in one hand and holds a large shield in the other. Beneath her feet lie wounded or dead Indian figures, depicted as rebels.
options.caption: “*Justice*” by John Tenniel, September 1857. Public domain.

choices.content: It portrays Britain’s violence as justified punishment for Indian rebellion.
It depicts Indians and Britons as equal participants in the conflict.
It criticizes Britain for using excessive violence during the rebellion.
It honors Indian leaders for resisting British colonialism.

assessment_items.question_content:
In 1900, Yaa Asantewaa, the Queen Mother of Ejisu in the Ashanti Empire (modern-day Ghana), led her people in a war against British colonial officials. When the British governor demanded the sacred Golden Stool—a symbol of Ashanti unity and leadership—Yaa Asantewaa rallied men and women to fight, declaring that if the men would not stand up to the British, the women would.

**Which of the following best explains Yaa Asantewaa’s leadership during the 1900 resistance against British rule?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She led her people to defend the Golden Stool as a symbol of independence and unity.
She fought to create a new Ashanti empire that would control all trade in West Africa.
She worked with British leaders to protect Ashanti trading rights.
She tried to convert the Ashanti to Christianity to resist British culture.
